\title{Comparing Static and Adaptive Safety Logic in\\
Human--Robot Ceiling Panel Installation}

\author{\IEEEauthorblockN{Zenan Wu, Luke Siniakov, Michael Magila}
\IEEEauthorblockA{Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering\\
Monash University, Melbourne, Australia}
\thanks{Final Year Project, 2026. Supervisor: Yizhe (Will) Wang.}}

\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
% STUB. State: the problem (fixed protective-separation zones stall collaborative
% work), the contribution (a certified speed-and-separation ENVELOPE that permits a
% learned Recognise--Predict--Adapt layer to add caution but never remove it), and
% the headline result (interruption burden reduced at equal or better hazard
% response, with the envelope guaranteeing the safety floor). Numbers reported here
% come from lab pilot data, not the synthetic pipeline-validation trace.
\end{abstract}

\begin{IEEEkeywords}
Human--robot collaboration, speed and separation monitoring, ISO/TS~15066, runtime
assurance, safety shielding, layered hidden Markov model, construction robotics.
\end{IEEEkeywords}

\section{Introduction}
% STUB. Motivate overhead ceiling-panel installation as a genuinely collaborative,
% ergonomically punishing task. Fixed-zone speed-and-separation monitoring is safe
% but blunt: it slows or stops the robot for a stationary worker who poses no
% closing hazard, taxing throughput. Contribution list:
% (1) a dynamic SSM ENVELOPE using measured approach speed as a certified floor;
% (2) a learned state + horizon-prediction layer shielded by that floor;
% (3) a three-rung evaluation (fixed-zone / dynamic-SSM / full) with a replay-based
%     ablation and a distractor protocol that reports specificity, not just
%     sensitivity.

\section{Related Work}
% STUB. ISO 10218 / ISO/TS 15066 collaborative-robot safety modes~\cite{iso10218_1,
% iso15066}; speed and separation monitoring metrics and
% implementations~\cite{marvel2013ssm,marvel2017implementing,byner2019dynamic};
% dynamic safety control~\cite{zanchettin2016safety}; human motion
% prediction~\cite{mainprice2013prediction}; safe HRI
% surveys~\cite{lasota2017survey,villani2018survey}; runtime assurance / Simplex and
% shielding for learned components~\cite{sha2001simplex,alshiekh2018shielding};
% layered HMMs and activity recognition~\cite{oliver2004lhmm,rabiner1989,lara2013har}.

\section{System Architecture}
\label{sec:arch}
% STUB. The Recognise -> Predict -> Adapt pipeline, then the sem-2 additions.

\subsection{Safety Envelope (Certified Floor)}
% STUB. The dynamic SSM stop distance with MEASURED approach speed:
The protective stop distance uses the measured closing speed $v_{\text{proj}}$
rather than a fixed worst-case human speed:
\begin{equation}
S(t) = \max(0,\, v_{\text{proj}}(t))\,T + C + S_a,
\label{eq:envelope}
\end{equation}
and maps the gap $d - S$ to a maximum permissible speed fraction (0 at/inside $S$,
ramping to 1 across a band). The learned layer may only reduce the command below
this floor; a recognition error can therefore only add caution, never speed.

\subsection{Horizon Prediction}
% STUB. Constant-acceleration time-to-breach fused with the state posterior.
The anticipation signal is kinematic, not label-based: a constant-acceleration
extrapolation gives the time until the operator crosses the red radius, clamped to a
human-plausible acceleration to reject sensor noise, and gated on genuine closing
motion. It is fused with the recognised hazard posterior by
$\text{risk} = \max(p_{\text{hazard}}, \text{imminence})$.

\section{Experimental Method}
\label{sec:method}
% STUB. Physical cell, motion capture, the Multi-Point Alignment \& Tool-Retrieval
% loop, cued slips, and the three controllers under comparison.

\subsection{Three Rungs}
% STUB. Fixed-zone (deployed practice), dynamic-SSM (standards rung), full adaptive.

\subsection{Distractor Protocol and Metrics}
% STUB. Cued distractors (fast lateral dart, fast retreat) are ground-truth
% non-hazard; they let us report hazard SPECIFICITY alongside sensitivity. The
% primary efficiency outcome is the interruption burden: the time-integrated speed
% deficit over ground-truth-safe periods (excluding hazard windows and red-zone
% occupancy), scored identically for every controller.

\section{Discussion}
% STUB. What each ingredient buys (from the ablation): speed-awareness (rung 1->2)
% recovers throughput for stationary workers; the state layer trades throughput for
% contextual caution; horizon prediction provides anticipation lead time. The
% envelope guarantees the floor regardless of the learned layer's correctness.

\section{Ethics and Safety}
% STUB. Cued slips and distractors, e-stop, supervision, lightweight surrogate
% panel, and the fact that the red-zone protective stop is active in BOTH
% conditions so no condition is less safe. See the ethics application.
